article.title: Apple to Open Siri to Rival AI Assistants With New Extensions System in iOS 27
article.summary: Apple is building an Extensions system that will let users route Siri queries to ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, and other third-party AI services starting with iOS 27.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Apple is developing a new "Extensions" system for Siri that will allow third-party AI chatbots to integrate directly with the voice assistant, ending the exclusive arrangement with OpenAI's ChatGPT that has been in place since iOS 18. The feature is expected to ship as part of iOS 27, iPadOS 27, and macOS 27, with an unveiling planned for Apple's Worldwide Developers Conference on June 8, according to [Bloomberg](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-26/apple-plans-to-open-up-siri-to-rival-ai-assistants-beyond-chatgpt-in-ios-27).

Under the new system, users who have Google Gemini, Anthropic's Claude, or other compatible AI apps installed will be able to send queries to those services from within Siri, rather than being limited to ChatGPT. The shift positions the iPhone as a multi-provider AI hub and represents Apple's most significant concession that no single AI service can serve all user needs.

This development follows [earlier reporting by The Machine Herald](/article/2026-03/10-apple-plans-to-replace-core-ml-with-core-ai-framework-at-wwdc-2026-signaling-a-developer-facing-ai-platform-shift) on Apple's plans to replace Core ML with a new Core AI framework, signaling a broader platform-level AI overhaul ahead of WWDC 2026.

## What We Know

Language discovered inside pre-release test builds describes the feature: "Extensions allow agents from installed apps to work with Siri, the Siri app and other features on your devices," as reported by [Bloomberg](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-26/apple-plans-to-open-up-siri-to-rival-ai-assistants-beyond-chatgpt-in-ios-27). Users will be able to enable or disable individual AI services through a dedicated menu in the Apple Intelligence and Siri section of the Settings panel. A new App Store section will surface compatible AI applications, directing users to download additional providers.

The named services expected to participate include OpenAI's ChatGPT, Google's Gemini, and Anthropic's Claude, according to [CNBC](https://www.cnbc.com/video/2026/03/26/apple-plans-to-open-up-siri-to-rival-ai-assistants-in-ios-27-update.html). Other major AI platforms available as apps on Apple devices — including Perplexity, Microsoft Copilot, xAI's Grok, and Meta AI — could also integrate through the Extensions system, though Apple has not confirmed which providers will be supported at launch.

The Extensions feature is part of a broader Siri overhaul. A separate Bloomberg report from March 24 detailed plans for a standalone Siri app with conversation history, a redesigned interface that places Siri within the Dynamic Island, and new entry points such as "Ask Siri" buttons integrated throughout the operating system, according to [Bloomberg](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-03-24/ios-27-features-apple-ai-reboot-with-siri-app-new-interface-ask-siri-button). The standalone app will display past conversations in a list or grid format, with options to favorite, search, and initiate new chats, as reported by [9to5Mac](https://9to5mac.com/2026/03/24/apple-planning-standalone-siri-app-for-ios-27-and-macos-27-per-report/).

Separately, Apple's underlying Siri intelligence is being rebuilt using Google Gemini models — a partnership that remains distinct from the Extensions system. Gemini powers the conversational engine itself, while Extensions determine which third-party service handles queries that Siri routes externally.

## What We Don't Know

Apple has not disclosed whether all AI apps will automatically qualify for the Extensions system or whether providers must meet specific approval requirements. The company also has not clarified whether users will be able to choose a different AI service for each individual request or must set a single default alternative to Siri's built-in responses.

The business terms for third-party AI providers remain unclear. Apple could generate revenue through its standard App Store commission on subscriptions to services like ChatGPT Plus or Claude Pro, but whether additional licensing or revenue-sharing arrangements are involved has not been reported. AI companies will need to update their apps to support the new Extensions API before integration becomes functional, meaning the marketplace may not be fully populated on day one.

It also remains uncertain how deeply third-party assistants will be able to access on-device data and system features. Siri's built-in capabilities include controlling device settings, accessing personal data across apps, and performing system-level actions — whether Extensions providers will have comparable access or be limited to conversational queries has not been specified.

## Analysis

The move marks a strategic reversal for Apple, which has historically maintained tight control over its software ecosystem. By opening Siri to competitors, Apple implicitly acknowledges that its own AI capabilities have lagged behind rivals — a gap that became visible after the initial Apple Intelligence launch drew criticism for its limited feature set.

The Extensions approach mirrors Apple's broader platform strategy: rather than building the best AI assistant itself, it provides the distribution layer and takes a cut of the resulting transactions. This is the same playbook Apple used with the App Store, Apple Pay, and its search engine deal with Google. For AI providers, access to Siri represents a distribution channel reaching more than two billion active Apple devices worldwide.

The timing aligns with a broader push by Apple to reposition itself in the AI race ahead of WWDC 2026. Together with the reported Core AI framework for developers and the Gemini-powered Siri backend, the Extensions system suggests Apple is building a layered AI strategy: proprietary on-device intelligence, a Google-powered conversational engine, and an open marketplace for third-party AI services.
